function.name: mcp_brave_search_brave_local_search
function.description:
Brave Local Search API provides enrichments for location search results. Access to this API is available only through the Brave Search API Pro plans; confirm the user's plan before using this tool (if the user does not have a Pro plan, use the brave_web_search tool). Searches for local businesses and places using Brave's Local Search API. Best for queries related to physical locations, businesses, restaurants, services, etc.
    
    Returns detailed information including:
        - Business names and addresses
        - Ratings and review counts
        - Phone numbers and opening hours

    Use this when the query implies 'near me', 'in my area', or mentions specific locations (e.g., 'in San Francisco'). This tool automatically falls back to brave_web_search if no local results are found.

country.description: Search query country, where the results come from. The country string is limited to 2 character country codes of supported countries.

search_lang.description: Search language preference. The 2 or more character language code for which the search results are provided.

ui_lang.description: The language of the UI. The 2 or more character language code for which the search results are provided.

count.description: Number of results (1-20, default 10). Applies only to web search results (i.e., has no effect on locations, news, videos, etc.)

safesearch.description: Filters search results for adult content. The following values are supported: 'off' - No filtering. 'moderate' - Filters explicit content (e.g., images and videos), but allows adult domains in search results. 'strict' - Drops all adult content from search results. The default value is 'moderate'.

freshness.description: Filters search results by when they were discovered. The following values are supported: 'pd' - Discovered within the last 24 hours. 'pw' - Discovered within the last 7 days. 'pm' - Discovered within the last 31 days. 'py' - Discovered within the last 365 days. 'YYYY-MM-DDtoYYYY-MM-DD' - Timeframe is also supported by specifying the date range e.g. [REDACTED_PHONE]to[REDACTED_PHONE].

text_decorations.description: Whether display strings (e.g. result snippets) should include decoration markers (e.g. highlighting characters).

function.name: mcp_brave_search_brave_news_search
function.description:
This tool searches for news articles using Brave's News Search API based on the user's query. Use it when you need current news information, breaking news updates, or articles about specific topics, events, or entities.
    
    When to use:
        - Finding recent news articles on specific topics
        - Getting breaking news updates
        - Researching current events or trending stories
        - Gathering news sources and headlines for analysis

    Returns a JSON list of news-related results with title, url, and description. Some results may contain snippets of text from the article.
    
    When relaying results in markdown-supporting environments, always cite sources with hyperlinks.
    
    Examples:
        - "According to [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/technology/china-bans/), China bans uncertified and recalled power banks on planes".
        - "The [New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2025/06/27/us/technology/ev-sales.html) reports that Tesla's EV sales have increased by 20%".
        - "According to [BBC News](https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-65910000), the UK government has announced a new policy to support renewable energy".

function.name: mcp_brave_search_brave_summarizer
function.description:
Retrieves AI-generated summaries of web search results using Brave's Summarizer API. This tool processes search results to create concise, coherent summaries of information gathered from multiple sources.

    When to use:

    - When you need a concise overview of complex topics from multiple sources
    - For quick fact-checking or getting key points without reading full articles
    - When providing users with summarized information that synthesizes various perspectives
    - For research tasks requiring distilled information from web searches

    Returns a text summary that consolidates information from the search results. Optional features include inline references to source URLs and additional entity information.

    Requirements: Must first perform a web search using brave_web_search with summary=true parameter. Requires a Pro AI subscription to access the summarizer functionality.

key.description: The key is equal to value of field key as part of the Summarizer response model.

entity_info.description: Returns extra entities info with the summary response.

inline_references.description: Adds inline references to the summary response.

function.name: mcp_context7_query_docs
function.description:
Retrieves and queries up-to-date documentation and code examples from Context7 for any programming library or framework.

You must call 'Resolve Context7 Library ID' tool first to obtain the exact Context7-compatible library ID required to use this tool, UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

Do not call this tool more than 3 times per question.

libraryId.description: Exact Context7-compatible library ID (e.g., '/mongodb/docs', '/vercel/next.js', '/supabase/supabase', '/vercel/next.js/v14.3.0-canary.87') retrieved from 'resolve-library-id' or directly from user query in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version'.

query.description: The question or task you need help with. Be specific and include relevant details. Good: 'How to set up authentication with JWT in Express.js' or 'React useEffect cleanup function examples'. Bad: 'auth' or 'hooks'. The query is sent to the Context7 API for processing. Do not include any sensitive or confidential information such as API keys, passwords, credentials, personal data, or proprietary code in your query.

function.name: mcp_context7_resolve_library_id
function.description:
Resolves a package/product name to a Context7-compatible library ID and returns matching libraries.

You MUST call this function before 'Query Documentation' tool to obtain a valid Context7-compatible library ID UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

Each result includes:
- Library ID: Context7-compatible identifier (format: /org/project)
- Name: Library or package name
- Description: Short summary
- Code Snippets: Number of available code examples
- Source Reputation: Authority indicator (High, Medium, Low, or Unknown)
- Benchmark Score: Quality indicator (100 is the highest score)
- Versions: List of versions if available. Use one of those versions if the user provides a version in their query. The format of the version is /org/project/version.

For best results, select libraries based on name match, source reputation, snippet coverage, benchmark score, and relevance to your use case.

Selection Process:
1. Analyze the query to understand what library/package the user is looking for
2. Return the most relevant match based on:
- Name similarity to the query (exact matches prioritized)
- Description relevance to the query's intent
- Documentation coverage (prioritize libraries with higher Code Snippet counts)
- Source reputation (consider libraries with High or Medium reputation more authoritative)
- Benchmark Score: Quality indicator (100 is the highest score)

Response Format:
- Return the selected library ID in a clearly marked section
- Provide a brief explanation for why this library was chosen
- If multiple good matches exist, acknowledge this but proceed with the most relevant one
- If no good matches exist, clearly state this and suggest query refinements

For ambiguous queries, request clarification before proceeding with a best-guess match.

IMPORTANT: Do not call this tool more than 3 times per question. If you cannot find what you need after 3 calls, use the best result you have.

query.description: The question or task you need help with. This is used to rank library results by relevance to what the user is trying to accomplish. The query is sent to the Context7 API for processing. Do not include any sensitive or confidential information such as API keys, passwords, credentials, personal data, or proprietary code in your query.

libraryName.description: Library name to search for and retrieve a Context7-compatible library ID. Use the official library name with proper punctuation — e.g., 'Next.js' instead of 'nextjs', 'Customer.io' instead of 'customerio', 'Three.js' instead of 'threejs'.

      Best for: Finding current information, news, facts, people, companies, or answering questions about any topic.

function.name: [REDACTED_TOKEN]
function.description:
A detailed tool for dynamic and reflective problem-solving through thoughts.
This tool helps analyze problems through a flexible thinking process that can adapt and evolve.
Each thought can build on, question, or revise previous insights as understanding deepens.

When to use this tool:
- Breaking down complex problems into steps
- Planning and design with room for revision
- Analysis that might need course correction
- Problems where the full scope might not be clear initially
- Problems that require a multi-step solution
- Tasks that need to maintain context over multiple steps
- Situations where irrelevant information needs to be filtered out

Key features:
- You can adjust total_thoughts up or down as you progress
- You can question or revise previous thoughts
- You can add more thoughts even after reaching what seemed like the end
- You can express uncertainty and explore alternative approaches
- Not every thought needs to build linearly - you can branch or backtrack
- Generates a solution hypothesis
- Verifies the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
- Repeats the process until satisfied
- Provides a correct answer

Parameters explained:
- thought: Your current thinking step, which can include:
  * Regular analytical steps
  * Revisions of previous thoughts
  * Questions about previous decisions
  * Realizations about needing more analysis
  * Changes in approach
  * Hypothesis generation
  * Hypothesis verification
- nextThoughtNeeded: True if you need more thinking, even if at what seemed like the end
- thoughtNumber: Current number in sequence (can go beyond initial total if needed)
- totalThoughts: Current estimate of thoughts needed (can be adjusted up/down)
- isRevision: A boolean indicating if this thought revises previous thinking
- revisesThought: If is_revision is true, which thought number is being reconsidered
- branchFromThought: If branching, which thought number is the branching point
- branchId: Identifier for the current branch (if any)
- needsMoreThoughts: If reaching end but realizing more thoughts needed

You should:
1. Start with an initial estimate of needed thoughts, but be ready to adjust
2. Feel free to question or revise previous thoughts
3. Don't hesitate to add more thoughts if needed, even at the "end"
4. Express uncertainty when present
5. Mark thoughts that revise previous thinking or branch into new paths
6. Ignore information that is irrelevant to the current step
7. Generate a solution hypothesis when appropriate
8. Verify the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
9. Repeat the process until satisfied with the solution
10. Provide a single, ideally correct answer as the final output
11. Only set nextThoughtNeeded to false when truly done and a satisfactory answer is reached

nextThoughtNeeded.description: Whether another thought step is needed

totalThoughts.description: Estimated total thoughts needed (numeric value, e.g., 5, 10)

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts), 'close' (close stdin/send EOF).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
For servers/watchers, do NOT use shell-level background wrappers (nohup/disown/setsid/trailing '&') in foreground mode. Use background=true so Hermes can track lifecycle and output.
After starting a server, verify readiness with a health check or log signal, then run tests in a separate terminal() call. Avoid blind sleep loops.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180, foreground max: 600). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily. Foreground timeout above 600s is rejected; use background=true for longer commands.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified exactly once when the process finishes. **This is the right choice for almost every long-running task** — tests, builds, deployments, multi-item batch jobs, anything that takes over a minute and has a defined end. Use this and keep working on other things; the system notifies you on exit. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with watch_patterns — when both are set, watch_patterns is dropped.

watch_patterns.description: Strings to watch for in background process output. HARD RATE LIMIT: at most 1 notification per 15 seconds per process — matches arriving inside the cooldown are dropped. After 3 consecutive 15-second windows with dropped matches, watch_patterns is automatically disabled for that process and promoted to notify_on_complete behavior (one notification on exit, no more mid-process spam). USE ONLY for truly rare, one-shot mid-process signals on LONG-LIVED processes that will never exit on their own — e.g. ['Application startup complete'] on a server so you know when to hit its endpoint, or ['migration done'] on a daemon. DO NOT use for: (1) end-of-run markers like 'DONE'/'PASS' — use notify_on_complete instead; (2) error patterns like 'ERROR'/'Traceback' in loops or multi-item batch jobs — they fire on every iteration and you'll hit the strike limit fast; (3) anything you'd ever combine with notify_on_complete. When in doubt, choose notify_on_complete. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with notify_on_complete — set one, not both.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits. Auto-runs syntax checks on .py/.json/.yaml/.toml and other linted languages; only NEW errors introduced by this write are surfaced (pre-existing errors are filtered out).

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

Create 4 high-priority blog posts for Nexa Paraguay in EN (English). Write as proper .mdx files with frontmatter. Real, publication-ready articles.

Files to create:

1. blog/en/uk-non-dom-abolition-paraguay.mdx
   Title: "UK Non-Dom Abolition: Your Paraguay Exit Strategy for 2026"
   Category: "UK Tax"
   Reading time: 10 min
   Target: UK high-net-worth individuals affected by non-dom abolition
   Content: Explain the change (April 2025, remittance basis gone, worldwide income taxed on arising). The 10-year IHT tail. 16,500 millionaires projected to leave. Compare options: Gibraltar (Cat 2, £42K cap), UAE (no citizenship, high CoL), Portugal (48% tax now). Paraguay: 0% foreign income, 3-year citizenship, 1 visit/3 years, $1,100-1,500/mo CoL. Concrete example of tax savings. End with CTA.

2. blog/en/paraguay-vs-portugal-2026.mdx
   Title: "Paraguay vs Portugal for UK Expats in 2026: The Honest Comparison"
   Category: "Comparison"
   Reading time: 11 min
   Target: UK expats comparing PY to Portugal
   Content: Portugal was the #1 choice until NHR died. Now foreign income taxed up to 48%. 183 days/year mandatory. 5 years to passport. Cost $2,500+/mo. Paraguay: 0% tax, 1 day/3 years, 3-year citizenship, $1,100-1,500/mo. Include a big comparison table. Address "but Portugal is Europe, EU passport" objection honestly. When PY makes sense vs when Portugal still makes sense.

3. blog/en/dac8-crypto-paraguay.mdx
   Title: "DAC8 is Live: Europe's Crypto Reporting Law and Why Paraguay Is the Escape Route"
   Category: "Crypto"
   Reading time: 8 min
   Target: European crypto holders (49M+)
   Content: DAC8 goes live Jan 2026. All EU crypto exchanges report holdings and transactions to tax authorities. Netherlands taxes unrealised crypto gains at 36% (Box 3). Germany 26.375% on gains held <1yr. Spain up to 28%. Paraguay: crypto = goods for natural persons, 0% CGT, no CRS, no wealth tax. Paraguay's DNIT Resolution 47/2026 is reporting-only, not a tax. X4T exchange licensed in PY, Ueno bank crypto-friendly. Concrete example of tax savings.

4. blog/en/us-paraguay-feie-tax-strategy.mdx
   Title: "FEIE + Paraguay: How Americans Can Earn Up to $132,900 Tax-Free in 2026"
   Category: "US Tax"
   Reading time: 9 min
   Target: US expats, FIRE community, digital nomads
   Content: FEIE allows $132,900 exclusion (2026). But you still need to live in a country with 0% local tax to maximize. Paraguay's territorial system = 0% on foreign income. Combined strategy: earn income through foreign corporation or as independent contractor → qualify for FEIE → PY 0% local tax → near-zero effective rate. Address FBAR/FATCA requirements. Compare to: Costa Rica (183 days/yr), Panama ($300K investment), Portugal (48% now). Include concrete example: single filer earning $120K saves ~$26K/yr in US tax + $0 PY tax.

For all posts: frontmatter with title, slug, date: "[REDACTED_PHONE]", author: "Nexa Paraguay", category, excerpt (2 sentences), coverImage: "/images/blog/default-cover.webp", readingMinutes, draft: false. Conversational tone with concrete numbers. End with soft CTA.

- **Framework:** `@ai-whisperers/*` package ecosystem (client imports via npm `file:` links)

## Introduction

Every foreigner who arrives in Paraguay with relocation plans hits the same wall: opening a local bank account. It sounds trivial — you walk in, show your passport, walk out with a debit card. In Paraguay, that is not how it works. The banking system is conservative, compliance-driven, and deeply relationship-based. This article explains what actually happens, why it is difficult, and how the process works when done properly.

## Why Is It So Hard?

Paraguayan banks operate under strict anti-money laundering regulations supervised by the SEPRELAD. For a foreigner with no local credit history, no local ID, and no proven income stream in the country, the risk classification is automatically high. Banks respond by demanding layers of documentation that most foreign applicants do not have on hand.

A typical request list includes: certified copy of your passport with entry stamp, proof of residence in your home country, a reference letter from your home bank (often apostilled), proof of income or net worth, your local tax ID (RUC), and a utility bill showing a Paraguayan address. Gathering these from abroad takes weeks.

## Which Banks Work for Foreigners?

Not all banks are equally open to foreign clients. Itaú Paraguay, Banco Continental, and Sudameris have the most experience with international clients, but each has different risk appetites. Some require a minimum deposit of USD 5,000 to USD 25,000. Others will not open an account for a non-resident at all without a local company already registered.

The key variable is whether you already have your temporary residency or at minimum your cedula (local ID) in progress. Without one of these, your options narrow significantly.

## The Role of a Local Introducer

This is the most underestimated factor. A personal introduction from a lawyer, accountant, or existing client who has a relationship with the bank manager changes everything. Paraguay is a relationship-driven economy. Walking in cold as a foreigner gets you the standard compliance treatment — slow, document-heavy, high chance of rejection. Walking in with a professional introducer from a known firm like Nexa Paraguay can reduce the process from weeks to days.

The Paraguayan bank appointment is the single step in our process that investors worry about most. Forums are full of horror stories — "I spent six weeks being passed between branches" or "The compliance officer rejected me without explanation." Those stories are real, but they're almost entirely the result of walking in cold, without a relationship, without prepared documentation, without a file already reviewed.

Here's what a coordinated bank appointment actually looks like when the paperwork is in order.

## Before the day

By the time you walk into the bank, three things are already true:

1. **The bank has seen your file.** We send it 10 days ahead through a named relationship contact — not a generic teller. That person has already flagged any compliance issues and come back with questions. When you arrive, you're not an unknown.
2. **Your documents are pre-approved in format.** Apostille, translations, income source evidence — the bank's compliance desk has green-lit the file form. You walk in with a sheet that says "everything checked."
3. **The specific KYC profile is agreed.** Paraguay's 2023 AML update (Resolución SEPRELAD 137) tightened source-of-funds documentation. We've supplied and matched the evidence to your declared profile ahead of the meeting.

This pre-work is the reason our bank appointments close in 60–90 minutes on the day. Without it, the same paperwork takes 4–6 weeks of back-and-forth.

## What the 60–90 minutes look like

**Minutes 0–10:** welcome, passport verification, file review with the named officer. You sign the KYC declaration.

## Introduction

The single biggest bottleneck in any Paraguay relocation is document preparation. You cannot expedite this step once you are in the country. Every missing apostille, every untranslated certificate, every document that needs re-issuing from your home country costs you weeks. This checklist is designed to be printed and checked off before you book your flight.

## Documents That Must Be Apostilled in Your Home Country

These documents require a Hague Apostille from the country where they were issued. This must be done before you leave. Paraguayan authorities will not accept documents without it.

| Document | Apostille Required | Sworn Translation Needed | Notes |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------------|-------|
| Birth certificate | Yes | Yes | Must be an official certified copy |
| Marriage certificate (if applicable) | Yes | Yes | Both civil and religious if applicable |
| Criminal record / police clearance | Yes | Yes | Must be issued within 90 days of travel |
| University degrees and transcripts | Yes | Yes | Needed if applying for professional visas |
| Bank reference letters | Yes | Yes | From your home bank, dated within 30 days |
| Proof of income or net worth | Yes | Yes | Tax returns, investment statements, or pension docs |

title: "UK Non-Dom Abolition: Your Paraguay Exit Strategy for 2026"
slug: "uk-non-dom-abolition-paraguay"
date: "[REDACTED_PHONE]"
author: "Nexa Paraguay"
category: "UK Tax"
excerpt: "The UK's non-dom regime ended in April 2025, taxing worldwide income on arising and triggering a 10-year IHT tail. Here's how Paraguay offers a clean exit with 0% foreign income tax, 3-year citizenship, and no physical presence requirement."
coverImage: "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/blog/default-cover.webp"
readingMinutes: 10

---

## The biggest UK tax shift in a generation

April 6, 2025, was the day the old UK non-dom regime died. After years of speculation, the remittance basis — the mechanism that let non-domiciled residents pay UK tax only on money they brought into the country rather than on their worldwide income — is gone.

The replacement is a residence-based system: if you live in the UK, your worldwide income is now taxed on an arising basis, regardless of where it was earned or where you consider your permanent home to be. For the roughly 68,800 individuals who claimed non-dom status, this is not a marginal change. It rewrites the tax calculation entirely.

## What actually changed

**Before April 2025:** Non-doms could elect the remittance basis and pay UK tax only on UK-source income and on foreign income they specifically brought into the country. Foreign income and gains kept offshore remained outside the UK tax net. The cost was an annual charge of £30,000 to £60,000 depending on how long you had been resident.

**After April 2025:** The remittance basis is abolished for everyone. All foreign income and gains are taxed on an arising basis, just like UK income. The first four years of UK residence get a partial exemption for new arrivals (the "FIG" regime — Foreign Income and Gains), but for existing non-doms, the transition is immediate and full.

The only saving grace in the transition is a limited rebasing option for capital assets held on April 5, 2019, and a two-year window ([REDACTED_PHONE]) to remit previously unremitted foreign income at a reduced 12% rate. After that, the door closes.

## The 10-year IHT tail

The inheritance tax changes are arguably the more consequential shift. Previously, non-doms were liable for IHT only on UK-situated assets. The new rules apply IHT based on a 10-year residence test: if you have been UK-resident for at least 10 of the last 20 tax years, your worldwide estate is subject to UK IHT at 40%.

Even after you leave the UK, the IHT tail follows you for 10 years. If you die within a decade of ceasing UK residence, your worldwide assets are still caught. Only after a full 10-year "wash-out" period do you shed the exposure.

This means moving to a zero-tax jurisdiction is not enough — you also need a country that will not reintroduce you to UK IHT through a double tax treaty or estate tax arrangement. Paraguay has no such treaty with the UK, and its territorial system means Paraguayan authorities do not tax or report foreign assets.

## 16,500 millionaires projected to leave

The numbers tell the story. According to the Henley Global Citizens Report and UBS analysis, an estimated 16,500 millionaires are expected to leave the UK in 2025 alone — a 157% increase from pre-reform levels. That is the highest outflow of any country in the world this year.

These are not people who made a casual decision. They are business owners, investment professionals, tech founders, and inherited-wealth families who have spent years or decades in the UK and are now confronting a tax bill that makes staying uneconomical.

## Your options: the shortlist

If you are a UK non-dom weighing an exit, you are probably looking at the same shortlist everyone else is. Here is how each one stacks up in 2026.

### Gibraltar — the familiar option

Gibraltar's Category 2 status remains popular because it is British, English-speaking, and geographically close. You pay tax on a capped £118,000 of worldwide income, with maximum liability of about £42,380 per year.

**The problem:** Gibraltar has no path to citizenship (it is a British Overseas Territory). Cost of living is comparable to London for housing — a two-bedroom apartment rents for £2,500–4,000 per month. And while the tax cap is attractive, it still means paying £42K/year for income above the threshold. For someone with £500K+ in annual income, that is a discount — but not a zero.

### UAE — the zero-tax icon

The UAE offers true 0% personal income tax, no capital gains tax, and no inheritance tax. It has been the destination of choice for high-net-worth individuals for a decade.

**The problem:** No path to citizenship (except by exceptional decree). Cost of living in Dubai or Abu Dhabi for a family — international school fees, premium housing, vehicles — runs $8,000–15,000 per month. The UAE also requires physical presence; you cannot hold a residency visa without being in-country regularly. And the heat and lifestyle are not for everyone.

### Portugal — the fallen favorite

Portugal was the #1 destination for UK expats until the Non-Habitual Resident (NHR) regime was abolished in January 2024. New residents are now taxed on worldwide income at progressive Portuguese rates up to 48%. The NHR 2.0 (IFICI) offers a 20% flat rate, but only on certain local-source employment and self-employment income — foreign investment income, dividends, and capital gains are taxed at standard progressive rates.

**The problem:** 48% on foreign income is worse than the UK. Portugal also requires 183 days of physical presence per year to maintain tax residency. And the 5-year path to citizenship through the D7 visa is becoming harder, with more scrutiny on applications.

### Paraguay — the emerging solution

Paraguay offers a combination that increasingly looks like the optimal answer for UK non-doms who want a genuine, long-term solution:

- **0% tax on foreign-source income.** Paraguay's territorial tax system taxes only income generated within Paraguay. Your foreign dividends, capital gains, rental income, interest, and business profits are all zero-taxed.
- **No physical presence requirement.** You need to visit Paraguay once every 3 years to renew residency. That is the entire requirement. No 183-day count, no minimum stay.
- **3-year path to citizenship.** After 3 years of temporary residency, you can apply for permanent residency, and immediately thereafter for naturalization. No language test, no investment minimum, no interview gauntlet.
- **Cost of living:** $1,100–1,500 per month for a single person, $2,000–3,000 for a family. A luxury apartment in Asuncion's best neighborhood rents for $800–1,200/month.
- **No CRS reporting with the UK.** Paraguay does not participate in the OECD's Common Reporting Standard with the UK. Your financial affairs stay private.

## Concrete example: the tax savings

Let's put numbers on it. Meet James — a 45-year-old UK non-dom who runs a consulting business that generates £400,000 per year in profit. He has a portfolio of foreign investments generating £80,000/year in dividends and interest. He has been UK-resident for 15 years.

**Under the old regime (pre-April 2025):**
James kept his foreign income offshore and remitted only what he needed. His UK tax was roughly £60,000/year on his consulting income after deductions, plus the £60,000 non-dom charge. Total: ~£120,000/year.

**Under the new regime (staying in the UK):**
James now pays UK tax on the full £480,000 worldwide. After the personal allowance taper, his effective rate is approximately 43% — roughly £206,000 in tax. Plus the IHT exposure on his £3M estate.

**In Paraguay:**
James establishes residency through Nexa Paraguay. His consulting income is structured through a Paraguayan company that invoices his foreign clients — 10% IRE tax on locally-sourced income. But his foreign dividends, interest, and capital gains? 0%. His total Paraguay tax bill: approximately £8,000/year on local-source consulting advisory. His effective savings vs staying in the UK: approximately £198,000 per year.

**The math on a 10-year horizon:**
- Stay in UK: £2,060,000 in tax + IHT on £3M estate = up to £3,260,000 total
- Move to Paraguay: ~£80,000 in tax over 10 years + no IHT exposure

That is a difference of over £3 million.

## Beyond the tax: quality of life

The common objection we hear is "but it's Paraguay — isn't it dangerous/underdeveloped/uncomfortable?" The honest answer is: Asuncion is not London, and it is not supposed to be. But it is also not what most people imagine.

Asuncion has modern shopping centers, excellent private healthcare (Hospital Sirio Libanes, San Roque), reliable high-speed internet in urban areas, and a growing international community. It has direct flights to Madrid (Air Europa), Sao Paulo, and Buenos Aires. The cost of living means you can have a housekeeper, a driver, and a country-club membership on a fraction of what you would spend on a modest London terrace.

For the non-dom who wants a genuine relocation — not a mailbox in Malta or an apartment in Dubai they visit twice a year — Paraguay offers a real life in a country where the tax code is actually on your side.

## The IHT workaround

The 10-year IHT tail is the single biggest trap for UK non-doms leaving the country. Even after you move, your worldwide estate is exposed to UK IHT at 40% if you die within 10 years of departure.

The solution involves a combination of:
1. Relocating to a territorial tax jurisdiction (Paraguay) where foreign assets are not reported
2. Restructuring asset ownership through offshore trusts or holding companies before departure
3. Using the 2-year transitional remittance window (April 2025 - April 2027) to clean up UK asset positions

Because Paraguay does not share financial information with HMRC under CRS, your offshore structures remain private. This is not about evasion — it is about legal tax planning in a jurisdiction that respects your privacy.

## The bottom line

The UK non-dom abolition is not a scare story. It is law. The remittance basis is gone, worldwide income is taxed on arising, and a 10-year IHT tail follows you even after you leave.

Gibraltar has a tax cap but costs almost as much as London. The UAE has zero tax but costs a fortune to live in and offers no citizenship. Portugal now taxes foreign income at up to 48%.

Paraguay offers 0% on foreign income, a 3-year path to citizenship, no physical presence requirement, and a cost of living that gives you financial freedom instead of just relocation. For UK non-doms who want a genuine, permanent solution — not a stopgap — it is the strongest option on the board in 2026.

---

title: "Paraguay vs Portugal for UK Expats in 2026: The Honest Comparison"
slug: "paraguay-vs-portugal-2026"
date: "[REDACTED_PHONE]"
author: "Nexa Paraguay"
category: "Comparison"
excerpt: "Portugal was the #1 destination for UK expats until NHR died. Now foreign income is taxed up to 48% and you need 183 days/year in-country. Here's how Paraguay compares on tax, residency, cost, and citizenship — and when each one actually makes sense."
coverImage: "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/blog/default-cover.webp"
readingMinutes: 11
draft: false
---

## The comparison that every UK expat is making

If you are a UK expat looking for a new base in 2026, you have already compared Paraguay and Portugal. They are the two most-discussed destinations in expat forums, WhatsApp groups, and advisor calls. And for good reason — both offer routes to residency, both are considered safe, and both are more affordable than the UK.

But the comparison has changed radically in the last 18 months. Portugal killed its Non-Habitual Resident (NHR) regime in January 2024. Paraguay has only gotten more accessible. The two countries are now in completely different tiers for most UK expats, and the right choice depends on knowing exactly what you are optimizing for.

This is the honest comparison — the one that includes the numbers, the trade-offs, and the reasons you might still choose Portugal even though the math is worse.

## The 30-second summary

|  | Paraguay | Portugal |
|---|---|---|
| **Tax on foreign income** | 0% (territorial system) | Up to 48% (progressive IRS rates) |
| **Corporate tax** | 10% (IRE) | 21% (IRC) + surcharges |
| **Physical presence required** | 1 visit every 3 years | 183 days per year |
| **Path to citizenship** | 3 years | 5 years |
| **Language requirement** | None for residency | A2 Portuguese for citizenship |
| **Monthly cost of living (single)** | $1,100–1,500 | $2,500–3,500 |
| **Monthly cost of living (family)** | $2,000–3,000 | $4,000–6,000 |
| **Luxury apartment rental (3BR)** | $800–1,200/mo | $2,000–4,000/mo (Lisbon/Porto) |
| **EU passport** | No (Mercosur) | Yes |
| **CRS reporting with UK** | No | Yes |
| **IHT/estate tax exposure** | 0% (territorial) | 10% on Portuguese assets |
| **Banking difficulty** | Moderate (4-8 weeks) | Moderate (2-4 weeks) |
| **Healthcare quality** | Good private system | Excellent public + private |
| **International schools** | 3-4 quality options | Numerous |
| **English proficiency** | Limited outside business | High in cities |

## The big one: tax

This is where the debate starts and ends for most people. Portugal was the undisputed king of tax-efficient European residency for a decade because NHR gave you 0% on most foreign income for 10 years. That regime is dead. New applicants cannot access it.

### Portugal today (post-NHR)

As of January 2024, new tax residents in Portugal are taxed under standard IRS rules. That means progressive rates from 14.5% to 48% on your worldwide income. The new NHR 2.0 (called IFICI) offers a flat 20% rate — but only on qualifying local-source employment and self-employment income in specific professions (academia, tech, research). It does not cover investment income, dividends, capital gains, rental income, or pension income.

For the typical UK expat — remote worker, investor, or pensioner — your foreign income is taxed at progressive Portuguese rates. Here is what that looks like in practice:

- **On £60,000/year foreign income:** approximately £13,500–16,000 in Portuguese tax
- **On £120,000/year foreign income:** approximately £36,000–42,000 in Portuguese tax
- **On £250,000/year foreign income:** approximately £90,000–115,000 in Portuguese tax

And Portugal does share data with HMRC under the Common Reporting Standard. Your financial picture is transparent.

### Paraguay today

Paraguay taxes only income generated within its territory. Full stop. If you earn money outside Paraguay — whether it is a remote salary, consulting fees, dividends, capital gains, crypto profits, rental income from a UK property, or pension — you pay 0% Paraguayan tax.

Your tax liability in Paraguay is limited to:

- **IRE (Corporate Income Tax):** 10% on locally-sourced business profits, if you operate through a Paraguayan entity
- **IRP (Personal Income Tax):** 10% on locally-sourced employment income
- **IVA (VAT):** 10% on goods and services consumed in Paraguay

If your income structure is clean — foreign clients, foreign investments, foreign property — your effective tax rate in Paraguay is 0%.

## The physical presence requirement

This is the second-biggest differentiator after tax, and it is often the deciding factor.

**Portugal:** You must spend 183 days per year in the country to maintain tax residency. This is not flexible. If you travel frequently, maintain a home elsewhere, or have business commitments outside Europe, Portugal is effectively a full-time residence. You cannot hold Portuguese residency while living primarily in another country.

**Paraguay:** You need to visit Paraguay once every 3 years to renew your residency card. That is it. You can live anywhere in the world, maintain your home country residence, travel freely, and keep Paraguayan residency active. Paraguay is the only country in the world with a significant tax advantage that offers this level of flexibility.

If you want to maintain a base in the UK, spend time in Asia, travel Latin America, or keep multiple residences — Paraguay works. Portugal does not.

## Cost of living: the real numbers

We see a lot of inflated numbers from relocation consultants on both sides. Here are the real figures from clients living in both countries as of early 2026.

**Single person, comfortable lifestyle (not frugal):**
- Paraguay (Asuncion): $1,100–1,500/month
- Portugal (Lisbon): $2,500–3,500/month
- Portugal (interior/Algarve off-season): $2,000–2,800/month

**Family of 4, good international school, car, housekeeper:**
- Paraguay (Asuncion): $2,000–3,000/month
- Portugal (Cascais/Estoril): $5,000–8,000/month

**Housing specifically:**
- Paraguay: Luxury 3BR in Villa Morra or Carmelitas: $800–1,200/month
- Portugal: Equivalent in Lisbon's best neighborhoods: $2,500–4,500/month

The difference is structural. Portugal adopted the euro and experienced 15 years of real estate inflation driven by foreign demand. Paraguay's real estate market is still local-priced. That gap will narrow over time, but for 2026, the cost advantage for Paraguay is roughly 2x.

## Path to citizenship

**Portugal:** 5 years of residency, then A2 Portuguese language test, then application. The waiting period after application has stretched to 2-3 years in practice. Total timeline: 7-8 years to passport.

**Paraguay:** 3 years of temporary residency, then permanent residency, then naturalization. No language test. No investment minimum. No interview process. Total timeline: 3-4 years to passport.

The Paraguayan passport provides visa-free or visa-on-arrival access to approximately 140 countries, including the Schengen Area, UK, Japan, Singapore, and most of Latin America through Mercosur.

## The EU passport question

This is the one objection we hear most often: "But Paraguay is not Europe. Portugal gives me an EU passport."

It is a valid point. If your primary goal is holding an EU passport — not tax optimization, not cost savings, not flexibility — then Portugal is still the choice. An EU passport gives you the right to live, work, and retire in any of 27 countries. That has real value, especially for families who want their children to have European options.

But the honest question is: what is that EU passport actually costing you?

If you earn £80,000/year from remote work and investments, choosing Portugal over Paraguay costs you approximately:

- **£16,000–20,000/year in extra tax** (Portuguese progressive rates vs 0% Paraguay)
- **£12,000–24,000/year in extra cost of living** (Portugal vs Paraguay)
- **5 years of your freedom to travel and live elsewhere** (183-day rule)

That is roughly £28,000–44,000 per year in additional cost, for 7-8 years, to get an EU passport. Over the full timeline, that is £200,000–350,000 total.

Is an EU passport worth that? For some people, absolutely. For others, it is a very expensive piece of paperwork.

There is also a middle path: get Paraguayan residency (and eventual citizenship) first, and move to Portugal later if the EU passport becomes a priority. Paraguayan citizens can apply for Portuguese residency through the normal process — nothing is foreclosed by choosing Paraguay first.

## When Paraguay makes sense

Paraguay is the better choice if you:

- Earn most of your income from foreign sources (remote work, consulting, investments, crypto, dividends)
- Want to minimize your tax burden — not just reduce it
- Value freedom of movement and do not want to be tied to 183 days/year anywhere
- Have a family and want to stretch your income further
- Are under 50 and want a 3-year path to a second passport
- Want privacy — no CRS reporting with the UK or EU
- Plan to maintain multiple residences

## When Portugal still makes sense

Portugal is still the better choice if you:

- Absolutely must have an EU passport and are willing to pay for it
- Plan to actually live full-time in Europe for the foreseeable future
- Have local-source income in Portugal (a Portuguese employer or business)
- Value the European lifestyle — cafes, beaches, proximity to other European capitals
- Are over 65 and primarily concerned with healthcare access and quality of life rather than tax
- Need English widely spoken in daily life

## The hybrid approach

Some of our most strategic clients do both. They establish Paraguayan residency first (fast, cheap, no physical presence required), use it to optimize their tax position immediately, and then apply for Portuguese residency or a D7 visa later if they decide they want the EU passport.

Because Paraguay has no physical presence requirement, you can hold both residencies simultaneously. You pay tax wherever you are tax-resident under the respective domestic laws, and Paraguay territorial system means your foreign income is already 0% there regardless.

This is not tax evasion. It is legal dual-residency planning, and it gives you optionality that choosing one country upfront does not.

## The bottom line

Portugal was the best deal in Europe for UK expats. That deal ended in 2024. The Portugal of 2026 taxes your foreign income at up to 48%, requires you to live there more than half the year, costs $2,500+/month, and takes 7+ years to deliver a passport.

Paraguay taxes your foreign income at 0%, requires one visit every 3 years, costs $1,100–1,500/month, and delivers citizenship in 3 years.

For most UK expats who are optimizing for financial freedom and flexibility, the choice is clear. For those who value the EU passport above all else, Portugal still has its place — but at a very high price.

---

**Not sure which path is right for you?** Book a free, no-obligation strategy call with Nexa Paraguay. We work with UK expats daily and can give you a clear, honest assessment of your options — including whether Paraguay, Portugal, or a hybrid approach makes sense for your specific situation.

[Book your free consultation] → https://nexa.paragu-ai.com/contacto

title: "DAC8 is Live: Europe's Crypto Reporting Law and Why Paraguay Is the Escape Route"

## The day crypto transparency went global

January 1, 2026. That is the date the EU's DAC8 directive came into effect, and it changes everything for the 49 million crypto holders across Europe.

DAC8 is the eighth iteration of the EU's Directive on Administrative Cooperation, and it is the first one designed specifically for crypto assets. From January 2026 onward, every crypto exchange, wallet provider, and broker operating in the EU is required to collect and report detailed transaction data to their national tax authority — including your identity, transaction history, holdings, and realized gains.

The reporting flows automatically through the EU network and, under the OECD's Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework (CARF), will be shared with tax authorities in over 50 jurisdictions by 2027.

If you hold crypto in Europe, your tax authority now knows exactly what you hold, what you traded, and what you owe.

## What DAC8 actually requires

Under DAC8, EU crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) must report for each customer:

- Full name, address, and tax identification number
- Type and amount of each crypto transaction
- Fair market value at the time of each transaction
- Transfer amounts and counterparty information

This applies to transactions in stablecoins, NFTs, DeFi tokens, and all other crypto assets. The only exemptions are for transactions under €1,000 and for assets that are demonstrably non-transferable (which is almost nothing in practice).

The data is reported annually to the national tax authority, which automatically exchanges it with other EU member states and, under CARF, with participating non-EU countries.

DAC8 is not retroactive — historical activity before January 1, 2026 is not reported. But from this year forward, your crypto life is transparent to your government.

## What this means for your tax bill

DAC8 does not create new taxes. It creates new reporting. But reporting is what enables enforcement, and enforcement is what turns theoretical tax rates into actual bills. Here is what European crypto holders are now facing:

**Netherlands (Box 3):** Unrealized crypto gains are taxed at a deemed return rate of approximately 36%, regardless of whether you sold. If your portfolio went up in value, you owe tax on the increase even if you did not cash out.

**Germany:** 26.375% capital gains tax on crypto held for less than one year. The holding period exemption (tax-free after 12 months) still applies — but only if you can prove the holding period to the tax authority, which now has your full transaction history.

**Spain:** Capital gains on crypto are taxed at progressive rates up to 28%. The "holding period" exemption does not exist. Every trade from one token to another is a taxable event.

**France:** Flat 30% flat tax (PFU) on crypto gains. No holding period exemption for crypto-to-crypto trades.

**Italy:** 26% capital gains tax on crypto profits exceeding €2,000 per year. New 2025 budget increased the rate to 33% for 2026.

**Belgium:** Speculative gains (holding period under 2 years) taxed at 33% + local surcharges.

If you are a European crypto holder who has been treating crypto as a long-term investment, DAC8 just made your cost of compliance — and your tax bill — significantly higher.

## The concrete problem

Let's make this real. Meet Anna, a 34-year-old software developer living in Berlin. She has been investing in crypto since 2020. Her current portfolio:

- 2.5 BTC (purchased at various prices between [REDACTED_PHONE])
- 12 ETH
- Various DeFi positions and altcoins totaling roughly €180,000 in current value

Total portfolio value: approximately €420,000.

Before DAC8, Anna reported her crypto on her tax return based on her own records. She tracked her trades manually and calculated her German CGT liability — 26.375% on gains from assets held less than one year. She paid roughly €8,000–12,000 per year in tax.

Under DAC8, her exchange is now reporting every single transaction automatically to the German Bundeszentralamt für Steuern. Any discrepancy between what Anna reports and what the exchange reports triggers an automatic audit flag. The cost of getting it wrong — penalties, interest, potential criminal investigation — is now existential.

Anna's options:
1. Stay in Germany, pay 26.375% CGT annually on short-term trades, and hope the holding period exemption continues to protect her long-term holdings
2. Move to a jurisdiction where crypto is not taxed and where her financial data is not shared with German authorities

## Paraguay: the crypto-friendly alternative

Paraguay has quietly become one of the most crypto-friendly jurisdictions in the world. Here is why.

### 1. Crypto = goods, not securities or currency

Paraguay's tax authority (SET) has classified cryptocurrency as goods for natural persons. This means buying, selling, and holding crypto is not a taxable event for individuals. There is no capital gains tax. There is no wealth tax on crypto holdings. There is no income tax on crypto gains.

For natural persons, crypto transactions are treated the same as buying and selling personal property — and since Paraguay's tax system is territorial, only transactions that occur within Paraguay are potentially taxable. If you buy crypto on an international exchange and hold it, Paraguay does not consider that a taxable event.

### 2. DNIT Resolution 47/2026: reporting, not taxation

In early 2026, Paraguay's tax authority issued DNIT Resolution 47/2026, which establishes a reporting framework for crypto transactions. The key insight: this is a **reporting resolution, not a taxing resolution**. It requires exchanges and certain businesses to report transactions — but it does not impose any tax on crypto gains for individuals.

This is the opposite of DAC8. DAC8 uses reporting to enable taxation. Paraguay uses reporting to monitor compliance with AML regulations, not to collect tax on crypto.

### 3. No CRS for crypto

Paraguay does not participate in the OECD's Common Reporting Standard for financial accounts. This means your Paraguayan bank account and your crypto holdings are not automatically reported to your home country's tax authority.

Combine this with Paraguay's territorial tax system, and the result is a jurisdiction where you can hold, trade, and transact in crypto without local taxation and without automatic information sharing with your home government.

### 4. Crypto-friendly banking exists

Ueno Bank, one of Paraguay's most innovative digital banks, has explicitly embraced crypto-friendly policies. Ueno accounts can receive transfers from crypto exchanges without the automatic freezing or account closure that plagues crypto holders in other jurisdictions.

X4T, a licensed crypto exchange operating in Paraguay, provides on-ramp and off-ramp services for converting crypto to fiat within the Paraguayan banking system. Having a locally licensed exchange means you are not operating in a gray area — you are using regulated infrastructure.

## The concrete savings

Let's go back to Anna. If she moves to Paraguay and establishes residency:

**In Germany (staying):**
- Portfolio: €420,000
- Annual trades: ~€80,000 in realized short-term gains
- German CGT at 26.375%: ~€21,100/year in tax
- Plus ongoing compliance costs, accountant fees, audit risk
- 10-year cost: ~€211,000 in tax + compliance

**In Paraguay (relocated):**
- Same portfolio: €420,000
- Same trades: ~€80,000 in realized gains
- Paraguayan CGT on crypto for natural persons: 0%
- Paraguay tax bill: €0
- 10-year cost: €0 in crypto tax

That is a €211,000 difference over 10 years — just on the crypto component of her finances. Add in the cost of living differential (Berlin: ~€2,200/month vs Asuncion: ~€1,000–1,400/month), and the total savings exceed €250,000 over the decade.

## The strategic play for European crypto holders

If DAC8 has you rethinking your residence, here is the most common structure we recommend for crypto holders moving to Paraguay:

1. **Establish Paraguayan residency** (8-12 weeks through Nexa Paraguay's process)
2. **Open a Ueno bank account** (crypto-friendly, USD and PYG accounts)
3. **Register with X4T or another local exchange** for fiat on-ramp/off-ramp
4. **Maintain your existing international exchange accounts** (Binance, Kraken, etc.) — your tax liability is determined by your residence, not where the exchange is based
5. **Transfer your tax residence to Paraguay** — from that point forward, your crypto transactions are under Paraguayan jurisdiction: 0% CGT for natural persons

The transition is clean. You are not hiding anything. You are legally changing your tax residence to a country that does not tax crypto gains.

## Risks and caveats

We believe in being honest about the risks:

- **Paraguay's crypto regulations are evolving.** DNIT Resolution 47/2026 is reporting-only today, but there is always a risk that future legislation could change the treatment of crypto for natural persons. The current political and economic consensus strongly favors maintaining the territorial system, but nothing is permanent.
- **Banking is not instant.** Opening accounts in Paraguay takes 4-8 weeks even with professional support. Plan ahead.
- **Liquidity is lower.** The Paraguayan crypto market is small. You will primarily use international exchanges and convert to fiat locally through X4T or similar services.
- **Spanish is required for most official processes.** While you can manage with English in the crypto community and at Nexa, dealing with banks and government agencies requires Spanish or a translator.

## The bottom line

DAC8 is live. Your crypto holdings are now visible to your tax authority. The era of self-reporting with limited enforcement is over.

European crypto holders face a choice: pay the tax in your current jurisdiction — with full transparency and automated enforcement — or relocate to a jurisdiction that aligns with your financial interests.

Paraguay offers 0% capital gains tax on crypto, no wealth tax, no CRS reporting, a 3-year path to citizenship, and a cost of living that makes the move financially positive from day one. For the 49 million crypto holders in Europe facing DAC8, it is the most attractive option on the board.
